content.question: Explain the difference between interfaces and type aliases in TypeScript. When would you use one over the other?
content.answer:
Both interfaces and type aliases define the shape of data, but they have different capabilities:

Interfaces:
- Can be extended (extends keyword)
- Support declaration merging (same name = merged)
- Best for object shapes and class contracts

Type Aliases:
- Can represent ANY type (unions, tuples, primitives, mapped types)
- Cannot be merged (duplicate = error)
- Best for unions, complex mapped types, utility types

Rule of thumb: Use interfaces for object shapes and public APIs, types for everything else.

codeExamples.title: Interface vs Type
codeExamples.explanation: Interfaces are best for defining object contracts. Type aliases are more flexible and can represent unions, tuples, and computed types.

codeExamples.title: Union & Discriminated Unions
codeExamples.explanation: Discriminated unions use a common 'kind' field to narrow types. TypeScript's control flow analysis provides type safety in each branch.

codeExamples.title: Intersection Types
codeExamples.explanation: Intersection types combine multiple types into one. The result must satisfy ALL constituent types.

notes: In practice, the interface vs type choice rarely matters for most code
React component props: both work, but interface is convention for public component APIs
Type aliases can use conditional types and mapped types - interfaces cannot
followUps: What is declaration merging and when is it useful?
Can a class implement a type alias?
What is the difference between extends and intersection?
interviewTips: Don't just say 'they're the same' - show you know the specific differences
Mention discriminated unions - it shows advanced TypeScript knowledge
Give a practical example of when you'd choose one over the other

content.question: Explain generics in TypeScript. Implement a type-safe function that works with any data type.
content.answer:
Generics let you write reusable code that works with any type while maintaining type safety. Instead of using 'any' (which disables type checking), generics preserve the actual type.

Key concepts:
- Type parameters: <T>, <K, V>
- Constraints: <T extends object>
- Default types: <T = string>
- Built-in utility types: Partial, Required, Pick, Omit, Record, Readonly

codeExamples.title: Generic Functions
codeExamples.explanation: Generics capture the actual type and carry it through. Constraints (extends) limit what types are acceptable.

codeExamples.title: Built-in Utility Types
codeExamples.explanation: Utility types transform existing types. They're essential for reducing duplication and keeping types in sync.

codeExamples.title: Implementing a Generic API Response
codeExamples.explanation: Generic response types ensure type safety across your entire API layer. Define once, reuse everywhere.

notes: Use constraints (extends) to limit generics instead of using 'any'
keyof T returns a union of all keys of T
Mapped types like Partial are built from: { [K in keyof T]?: T[K] }
followUps: How would you implement Partial from scratch?
What is a conditional type? Give an example.
What is the difference between unknown and any?
interviewTips: Show a before/after: 'any' version vs generic version - makes the value clear
Know at least 5 utility types and when to use each
The getProperty function with keyof is a classic interview question

content.question: What are type guards? How does TypeScript narrow types based on runtime checks?
content.answer:
Type guards are runtime checks that TypeScript uses to narrow a type within a conditional block. When you check a condition, TypeScript automatically narrows the type in that branch.

Built-in narrowing:
- typeof: for primitives (string, number, boolean)
- instanceof: for class instances
- in: for checking property existence
- Equality checks: === null, !== undefined

Custom type guards: functions that return 'value is Type'

codeExamples.title: Built-in Type Guards
codeExamples.explanation: TypeScript's control flow analysis automatically narrows types based on these checks. Each branch has a more specific type.

codeExamples.title: Custom Type Guard Functions
codeExamples.explanation: Custom type guards return 'value is Type'. Assertion functions use 'asserts value is Type' and throw if the assertion fails.

codeExamples.title: Exhaustive Check with never
codeExamples.explanation: Assigning to 'never' in the default case ensures all union members are handled. If you add a new shape variant, TypeScript will error here until you add a case for it.

notes: Type guards are essential for working with union types safely
The 'never' exhaustive check pattern catches missing cases at compile time
Custom type guards are useful when built-in checks aren't sufficient
followUps: What happens if you don't handle all cases in a discriminated union?
How is 'unknown' different from 'any' in terms of narrowing?
When would you use assertion functions vs type guard functions?
interviewTips: Show the exhaustive check pattern with never - it impresses interviewers
Explain that narrowing happens at compile time but checks happen at runtime
Know the difference: type guards return boolean, assertions throw on failure

content.question: Why would you choose TypeScript over JavaScript? What problems does TypeScript solve?
content.answer:
TypeScript is a statically typed superset of JavaScript that compiles to plain JS. It solves several major pain points in JavaScript development:

1. Catches bugs at compile time instead of runtime - typos, wrong argument types, missing properties are all caught before your code ever runs.

2. Self-documenting code - type annotations serve as living documentation. Function signatures tell you exactly what inputs are expected and what output is returned.

3. Superior IDE support - autocompletion, refactoring, go-to-definition, and inline errors are dramatically better with types.

4. Safer refactoring - when you change a type or interface, the compiler immediately shows every place that needs updating.

5. Gradual adoption - since TS is a superset of JS, you can adopt it incrementally. Any valid JS is valid TS.

codeExamples.title: Bugs Caught at Compile Time
codeExamples.output:
// JS: runtime crash - Cannot read properties of undefined
// TS: compile-time error - caught before code runs
codeExamples.explanation: TypeScript catches entire categories of bugs that JavaScript would only reveal at runtime, including typos, wrong types, and missing arguments.

codeExamples.title: Self-Documenting Function Signatures
codeExamples.explanation: Type annotations serve as always-up-to-date documentation. Unlike JSDoc comments, types are enforced by the compiler and cannot become stale.

codeExamples.title: Safe Refactoring
codeExamples.explanation: When you change a type, the compiler creates a checklist of every file that needs updating. This makes large refactors safe and complete.

notes: TypeScript adds zero runtime overhead - types are erased during compilation
All valid JavaScript is valid TypeScript (gradual adoption is easy)
The TypeScript compiler (tsc) can target any JS version (ES5, ES2020, ESNext)
followUps: What are the downsides of TypeScript?
How does TypeScript handle type erasure?
Can TypeScript prevent ALL runtime errors?
interviewTips: Focus on real-world benefits: fewer bugs, better refactoring, better IDE support
Mention gradual adoption - companies don't need to rewrite everything at once
Acknowledge trade-offs: build step, learning curve, verbose for small scripts

content.question: What are the basic types in TypeScript? Explain type annotations and type inference.
content.answer:
TypeScript provides several primitive types that map to JavaScript values, plus additional types for safer code:

Primitive types: string, number, boolean, null, undefined, bigint, symbol
Special types: void (no return), never (unreachable), unknown (safe any), any (escape hatch)

Type annotations explicitly declare a variable's type with a colon syntax. Type inference lets TypeScript automatically determine the type from the assigned value. Best practice: let TypeScript infer when obvious, annotate when the type isn't clear from context (function parameters, return types, complex expressions).

codeExamples.title: Primitive Types and Annotations
codeExamples.explanation: Use annotations when the type isn't obvious. Let TypeScript infer when the value makes the type clear. const declarations get narrower literal types.

codeExamples.title: Function Type Annotations
codeExamples.explanation: Always annotate function parameters. Return types can often be inferred, but explicit annotations catch accidental return type changes.

codeExamples.title: Special Types: void, never, unknown
codeExamples.explanation: Prefer unknown over any when the type is truly unknown. unknown forces you to narrow before use, keeping your code safe.

notes: TypeScript's type inference is powerful - you don't need to annotate everything
Use strict mode (strict: true in tsconfig) to catch null/undefined errors
never is used for exhaustive checks and functions that never return
followUps: When should you use explicit annotations vs letting TypeScript infer?
What is the difference between null and undefined in TypeScript?
Why is 'any' considered harmful?
interviewTips: Show you understand inference - don't over-annotate in your code examples
Demonstrate the difference between void, never, and undefined
Mention strict mode and why it matters for production code

content.question: Explain arrays, tuples, and enums in TypeScript. When would you use each?
content.answer:
Arrays, tuples, and enums are three fundamental collection/value types in TypeScript:

Arrays: Ordered collections of a single type (or union). Use number[] or Array<number> syntax. Best for lists of homogeneous items.

Tuples: Fixed-length arrays where each position has a specific type. Use [string, number] syntax. Best for returning multiple values from functions or representing structured pairs.

Enums: Named sets of constants. Can be numeric (default) or string-based. Best for a fixed set of known values. Consider using string literal unions as a lighter alternative.

codeExamples.title: Arrays and Readonly Arrays
codeExamples.explanation: Use readonly arrays for function parameters to prevent accidental mutation. TypeScript correctly infers return types of array methods.

codeExamples.title: Tuples
codeExamples.explanation: Tuples are perfect for functions returning multiple values (like React's useState). Named elements improve readability without runtime cost.

codeExamples.title: Enums vs String Literals
codeExamples.explanation: String enums are readable but add runtime overhead. String literal unions are often preferred in modern TypeScript for their simplicity and better tree-shaking.

notes: Prefer string literal unions over enums in most cases for lighter output
Use readonly arrays in function parameters to prevent accidental mutation
Tuples are structurally typed - TypeScript checks length and position types
followUps: What is the difference between const enum and regular enum?
How do you enforce tuple immutability?
When would you prefer an enum over a string literal union?
interviewTips: Mention the enum vs string literal trade-off - it shows modern TS awareness
Show named tuple elements - most candidates don't know about this feature
Demonstrate readonly arrays as a best practice for function parameters

content.question: How do you type functions in TypeScript? Explain function overloads and when you'd use them.
content.answer:
TypeScript provides multiple ways to type functions: inline annotations, type aliases, interfaces, and overload signatures.

Function overloads let you define multiple call signatures for a single function. The compiler picks the matching signature based on the arguments. This is useful when a function behaves differently based on input types.

Key concepts:
- Overload signatures (declarations) describe the public API
- Implementation signature must be compatible with all overloads
- The implementation signature is NOT directly callable
- Generic functions are often a cleaner alternative to overloads

codeExamples.title: Function Type Annotations
codeExamples.explanation: Type aliases make function types reusable. Always type callback parameters to ensure callers pass the right function shape.

codeExamples.title: Function Overloads
codeExamples.explanation: Overloads provide precise return types based on input. The implementation must handle all overload cases but its signature is hidden from callers.

codeExamples.title: Generic Functions vs Overloads
codeExamples.explanation: Prefer generics when the relationship between input and output is consistent. Use overloads only when the return type genuinely differs based on specific inputs.

notes: Function overloads are resolved top-to-bottom; put more specific signatures first
The implementation signature must be a superset of all overload signatures
Generics are usually cleaner than overloads for simple type mapping
followUps: When would you use overloads vs generics vs union types?
How does TypeScript handle the 'this' keyword in functions?
What is a constructor signature in TypeScript?
interviewTips: Show a real-world overload example like createElement or event handlers
Explain why the implementation signature is hidden from callers
Mention that overloads should be a last resort - generics are usually better

content.question: How do you define object types in TypeScript? Explain optional properties, readonly, and excess property checking.
content.answer:
TypeScript uses structural typing (duck typing) for objects - if an object has the required shape, it is compatible with the type, regardless of its declared name.

Key features:
- Optional properties (?): can be present or absent
- Readonly properties: cannot be reassigned after creation
- Excess property checking: TypeScript catches extra properties in object literals
- Structural compatibility: types are compatible based on shape, not name

This system provides flexibility while maintaining safety. Understanding structural typing is essential because it differs from nominal typing used in Java or C#.

codeExamples.title: Object Types and Optional Properties
codeExamples.explanation: Optional properties are marked with ?. Readonly prevents reassignment. Use Readonly<T> to make all properties readonly at once.

codeExamples.title: Structural Typing (Duck Typing)
codeExamples.explanation: Structural typing means any value with the required properties is compatible. Excess property checking only applies to object literals directly assigned.

codeExamples.title: Nested Objects and Utility Patterns
codeExamples.explanation: Combine interfaces with utility types for common patterns like partial updates or immutable data. DeepReadonly is a common custom utility type.

notes: Excess property checking only triggers on direct object literal assignment
Structural typing means a class instance satisfies an interface without 'implements'
Use Partial<T> for update DTOs and Required<T> when you need all fields
followUps: What is excess property checking and when does it apply?
How does structural typing differ from nominal typing?
How would you implement a DeepPartial utility type?
interviewTips: Explain the variable-bypass of excess property checking - it's a common gotcha
Show you know structural vs nominal typing - it's a core TypeScript concept
Demonstrate DeepReadonly or DeepPartial to show advanced type-level programming

content.question: Explain union and intersection types. How do they differ and when would you use each?
content.answer:
Union types (A | B) represent values that can be ONE of several types. You can only access properties common to all members unless you narrow the type first.

Intersection types (A & B) combine multiple types into one that has ALL properties from every type. The result must satisfy every constituent type.

Key differences:
- Union: value is A OR B (wider, more flexible)
- Intersection: value is A AND B (narrower, more specific)
- Union of objects: only shared properties are accessible
- Intersection of objects: all properties from all types are accessible

Unions model alternatives. Intersections model composition/mixins.

codeExamples.title: Union Types in Practice
codeExamples.explanation: Unions with a discriminant field (like 'status') enable safe narrowing. Without narrowing, only shared properties are accessible.

codeExamples.title: Intersection Types for Composition
codeExamples.explanation: Intersections are ideal for composing reusable type fragments. Each fragment adds capabilities without modifying the original types.

codeExamples.title: Union vs Intersection Edge Cases
codeExamples.explanation: Intersecting primitive types or conflicting property types produces 'never'. Intersecting unions keeps only the shared members.

notes: Union of primitives: string | number means either type is valid
Intersection of primitives: string & number is 'never' (impossible)
Discriminated unions with a 'type' or 'kind' field are a key TypeScript pattern
followUps: What happens when you intersect two types with the same property but different types?
How do distributive conditional types work with unions?
When would you use a union of objects vs an interface with optional properties?
interviewTips: Draw the mental model: union = OR (wider), intersection = AND (narrower)
Show the discriminated union pattern - it's used constantly in real codebases
Mention the string & number = never case to show deep understanding

content.question: What are literal types in TypeScript? How does 'as const' work and when should you use it?
content.answer:
Literal types represent exact values rather than broad categories. Instead of 'string', a literal type is a specific string like 'hello'. TypeScript automatically infers literal types for const declarations.

'as const' (const assertion) tells TypeScript to infer the narrowest possible type for an expression:
- Strings/numbers become literal types instead of string/number
- Arrays become readonly tuples instead of mutable arrays
- Objects get readonly properties with literal types

This is incredibly useful for configuration objects, action types, and anywhere you want TypeScript to track exact values.

codeExamples.title: Literal Types
codeExamples.explanation: Literal types restrict values to exact matches. They are the foundation of discriminated unions and enable powerful pattern matching.

codeExamples.title: const Assertions (as const)
codeExamples.explanation: as const freezes values to their literal types and makes everything readonly. It's essential for deriving union types from objects.

codeExamples.title: Practical Patterns with as const
codeExamples.explanation: The 'as const + typeof + keyof' pattern is a popular alternative to enums. It provides the same type safety with less runtime overhead.

notes: as const makes everything deeply readonly and narrows to literal types
The typeof + keyof + as const combo creates union types from objects
Literal types are erased at runtime - they only exist during type checking
followUps: How does as const differ from Object.freeze()?
Can you use as const with function return values?
What is the satisfies operator and how does it work with as const?
interviewTips: Show the as const + typeof + keyof pattern for deriving union types
Compare const enum vs as const object - both have trade-offs
Mention that as const is compile-time only; Object.freeze is runtime

content.question: Explain the difference between unknown, any, and never in TypeScript. When should you use each?
content.answer:
These three types represent the extremes of TypeScript's type system:

'any' is the escape hatch - it disables ALL type checking. Any value can be assigned to 'any', and 'any' can be assigned to anything. Use it only for migration or truly dynamic scenarios.

'unknown' is the type-safe counterpart to 'any'. Any value can be assigned to 'unknown', but you MUST narrow it before using it. It forces you to check the type before performing operations.

'never' is the bottom type - it represents values that never occur. No value can be assigned to 'never'. It appears in exhaustive checks, functions that never return, and impossible intersections.

Type hierarchy: any/unknown (top) > all types > never (bottom).

codeExamples.title: any vs unknown
codeExamples.explanation: Use 'unknown' instead of 'any' for values from external sources (APIs, JSON, user input). It forces safe handling without disabling type checking.

codeExamples.title: never Type
codeExamples.explanation: never represents impossibility. It's essential for exhaustive checks (catching unhandled union members) and filtering types in conditional types.

codeExamples.title: Practical Comparison
codeExamples.explanation: In modern TypeScript, catch clauses default to 'unknown'. This forces you to check the error type before accessing properties, preventing runtime crashes.

notes: unknown is the type-safe top type; any is the unsafe top type; never is the bottom type
Prefer unknown over any in every case except JS migration or truly dynamic code
never is assignable TO everything but nothing is assignable TO never
followUps: Why did TypeScript introduce unknown if any already existed?
How does never interact with union and intersection types?
What is the difference between void and never?
interviewTips: Always recommend unknown over any - it shows you prioritize type safety
Show the exhaustive check pattern with never - it's a must-know
Mention that catch blocks use unknown by default in modern TypeScript

content.question: What are type assertions in TypeScript? When should you use them and what are the risks?
content.answer:
Type assertions tell TypeScript 'trust me, I know the type'. They override the compiler's inferred type without any runtime check. TypeScript uses the 'as' syntax (or angle-bracket syntax in non-JSX files).

Key assertion types:
- 'as Type': Override the inferred type (most common)
- '!' (non-null assertion): Tell TS a value is not null/undefined
- 'as const': Narrow to the most specific literal type
- 'satisfies': Validate a type while keeping the narrow inferred type

Assertions are NOT type casts - they perform zero runtime conversion. They simply tell the compiler to treat a value as a different type. Incorrect assertions lead to runtime errors.

codeExamples.title: Type Assertions with 'as'
codeExamples.explanation: Type assertions override the compiler but add zero runtime safety. Use them sparingly and only when you have knowledge the compiler lacks.

codeExamples.title: Non-Null Assertion (!)
codeExamples.explanation: The ! operator suppresses null/undefined checks. Prefer narrowing (if checks) or optional chaining (?.) over non-null assertions for safer code.

codeExamples.title: satisfies Operator (TS 4.9+)
codeExamples.explanation: satisfies validates that a value matches a type without widening it. Combined with as const, it gives you both validation and narrow literal types.

notes: Assertions are compile-time only - they add zero runtime safety
Prefer narrowing (if/typeof/instanceof) over assertions whenever possible
satisfies is the modern best practice: validates without widening
followUps: When is a double assertion (as unknown as T) acceptable?
How does satisfies differ from a type annotation?
What are the risks of non-null assertions in production code?
interviewTips: Emphasize that assertions are NOT runtime casts - this is a common misconception
Show satisfies as a modern alternative - it demonstrates up-to-date TS knowledge
Mention that excessive assertions often indicate a design problem

content.question: What are discriminated unions? How do you implement them and ensure exhaustive handling?
content.answer:
Discriminated unions (also called tagged unions or algebraic data types) are union types where each member has a common property with a unique literal value. This 'discriminant' property lets TypeScript narrow the type in conditional branches.

Three requirements:
1. A common property present in all members (the 'tag' or 'discriminant')
2. Each member has a unique literal type for that property
3. A type alias that unions all members

Discriminated unions are one of TypeScript's most powerful patterns. They model state machines, API responses, events, and any scenario with distinct variants. Combined with exhaustive checking, they ensure you handle every case.

codeExamples.title: Basic Discriminated Union
codeExamples.explanation: The discriminant property ('ok' or 'status') lets TypeScript narrow to the exact variant in each branch. Properties unique to a variant are only accessible after narrowing.

codeExamples.title: Exhaustive Checking Pattern
codeExamples.explanation: The assertNever helper catches unhandled variants at compile time. When you add a new variant, TypeScript immediately tells you every switch that needs updating.

codeExamples.title: Real-World: Redux Actions & Event Handling
codeExamples.explanation: Discriminated unions are the backbone of type-safe state management. Each action variant carries exactly the data it needs, and TypeScript ensures correct access.

notes: The discriminant property must be a literal type (string, number, boolean literal)
You can use multiple discriminant properties for finer narrowing
Discriminated unions are TypeScript's version of algebraic data types from ML/Haskell
followUps: How do discriminated unions compare to class hierarchies?
Can you use multiple discriminant properties?
How would you extract a single variant from a discriminated union type?
interviewTips: Implement the assertNever exhaustive check - interviewers love this pattern
Show a real-world example like AsyncState or Redux actions
Explain that this pattern makes impossible states unrepresentable

content.question: What are mapped types? Show how to create custom utility types using mapped types.
content.answer:
Mapped types create new types by transforming each property of an existing type. They iterate over the keys of a type using the 'in keyof' syntax and produce a new type with transformed properties.

Syntax: { [K in keyof T]: NewType }

You can modify properties with:
- ? / -? : add or remove optionality
- readonly / -readonly : add or remove readonly
- 'as' clause: rename or filter keys

Built-in utility types like Partial, Required, Readonly, and Pick are all implemented using mapped types. Understanding mapped types lets you create custom utilities tailored to your codebase.

codeExamples.title: Implementing Built-in Utilities
codeExamples.explanation: Mapped types iterate over keys with [K in keyof T]. The ? and readonly modifiers can be added (+) or removed (-) to transform properties.

codeExamples.title: Custom Mapped Types
codeExamples.explanation: Key remapping with 'as' lets you rename keys using template literal types. Capitalize, Uppercase, Lowercase, and Uncapitalize are built-in string manipulation types.

codeExamples.title: Filtering and Conditional Mapped Types
codeExamples.explanation: Using 'never' in key remapping filters out unwanted keys. Conditional types in the 'as' clause enable powerful type-level filtering.

notes: Mapped types preserve the modifier state unless you explicitly change it
Key remapping (as clause) was introduced in TypeScript 4.1
Combine mapped types with conditional types for advanced type transformations
followUps: How would you create a DeepPartial type?
What is the difference between mapped types and conditional types?
How do you remove specific properties from a mapped type?
interviewTips: Be able to implement Partial and Pick from scratch - it's a common interview question
Show key remapping with template literals to demonstrate advanced knowledge
Explain the -? and -readonly modifier removal syntax

content.question: Explain conditional types and the 'infer' keyword. How do you use them to extract and transform types?
content.answer:
Conditional types select one of two types based on a condition, using the syntax: T extends U ? X : Y (if T is assignable to U, resolve to X, else Y).

The 'infer' keyword lets you extract a type from within a pattern. It works like a type-level variable that captures part of a type during pattern matching.

Key behaviors:
- Distributive: when applied to a union, the conditional distributes over each member
- infer: captures a type variable from within a conditional check
- Nested: conditional types can be chained for complex logic

Built-in types like ReturnType, Parameters, and Awaited are all conditional types with infer.

codeExamples.title: Basic Conditional Types
codeExamples.explanation: Conditional types distribute over union members by default. Each member is checked individually, and results are unioned. Wrap in tuple [T] to prevent distribution.

codeExamples.title: infer Keyword - Type Extraction
codeExamples.explanation: infer declares a type variable that TypeScript fills in during pattern matching. It's like destructuring but for types.

codeExamples.title: Advanced Conditional Patterns
codeExamples.explanation: Conditional types with infer enable powerful type-level pattern matching. You can decompose tuples, extract nested types, and create recursive type transformations.

notes: Conditional types distribute over unions unless wrapped in tuples [T]
infer can only be used in the extends clause of a conditional type
ReturnType, Parameters, ConstructorParameters, InstanceType all use infer internally
followUps: How does distributive behavior work with conditional types?
Can you use multiple infer keywords in a single conditional type?
How would you implement a type that extracts all string properties from an object?
interviewTips: Implement ReturnType from scratch - it's a classic advanced interview question
Show the distribution gotcha with unions and how to prevent it
Demonstrate recursive conditional types like DeepAwaited for bonus points

content.question: What are template literal types in TypeScript? How can you use them to create powerful string-based type patterns?
content.answer:
Template literal types use the same backtick syntax as JavaScript template literals but at the type level. They let you construct new string literal types by concatenating other string literals, unions, and type variables.

Combined with union types, they generate every permutation automatically. TypeScript also provides built-in string manipulation types: Uppercase, Lowercase, Capitalize, and Uncapitalize.

Template literal types are heavily used in library typings (e.g., event emitters, CSS-in-JS, routing) to provide autocomplete and type safety for string-based APIs.

codeExamples.title: Basic Template Literal Types
codeExamples.explanation: Template literal types combine string parts at the type level. When unions are involved, TypeScript generates every possible combination automatically.

codeExamples.title: Event Emitter Typing
codeExamples.explanation: Template literal types with mapped types let you generate method names from property names. This is how libraries provide type-safe APIs for string-based patterns.

codeExamples.title: String Parsing with infer
codeExamples.explanation: Combining template literals with infer lets you parse and decompose string types. This is the foundation for type-safe routing, dot-path accessors, and string-based DSLs.

notes: Template literal types generate all permutations when used with union types
Built-in string manipulation types: Uppercase, Lowercase, Capitalize, Uncapitalize
Combining template literals with infer enables type-level string parsing
followUps: How would you type a CSS-in-JS library using template literal types?
Can template literal types cause performance issues with large unions?
How do you extract route parameters from a URL pattern type?
interviewTips: Show the union permutation expansion - it demonstrates understanding of distributive behavior
The route parameter extraction example is very impressive in interviews
Mention real-world usage in libraries like Express route typing or event emitters

content.question: Explain the most commonly used built-in utility types in TypeScript. When and why would you use each one?
content.answer:
TypeScript ships with a rich set of utility types that transform existing types without creating new ones from scratch. They reduce boilerplate and keep types in sync with their source.

Property modifiers: Partial<T>, Required<T>, Readonly<T>
Property selection: Pick<T,K>, Omit<T,K>
Object creation: Record<K,V>
Union filtering: Extract<T,U>, Exclude<T,U>, NonNullable<T>
Function inspection: ReturnType<T>, Parameters<T>, ConstructorParameters<T>, InstanceType<T>
String manipulation: Uppercase<S>, Lowercase<S>, Capitalize<S>, Uncapitalize<S>

Knowing these avoids reinventing the wheel and signals TypeScript maturity to interviewers.

codeExamples.title: Property Modifier Utilities
codeExamples.explanation: Partial is the most commonly used utility type. It shines for update operations where only some fields change. Required is its inverse, making every field mandatory.

codeExamples.title: Selection & Creation Utilities
codeExamples.explanation: Pick and Omit derive subsets of a type. Record creates dictionary-like types. Use literal union keys with Record for exhaustive key coverage.

codeExamples.title: Union & Function Utilities
codeExamples.explanation: Extract and Exclude filter union members. ReturnType and Parameters inspect functions without calling them. These are essential for building type-safe wrappers.

notes: Partial and Pick are the two most frequently used utility types in real codebases
ReturnType<typeof fn> lets you derive a type from a function without defining it separately
Utility types compose: Partial<Pick<User, 'name' | 'email'>> makes a subset optional
followUps: How would you implement Omit using Pick and Exclude?
What is the difference between Extract and Pick?
How do you create a DeepPartial utility type?
interviewTips: Know how to implement at least Partial and Pick from scratch using mapped types
Show composition of utilities - e.g., Required<Pick<User, 'name' | 'email'>>
Mention ReturnType<typeof fn> as a way to keep types DRY without manual definitions

content.question: Explain the keyof and typeof operators in TypeScript. How do they work together for type-safe property access?
content.answer:
keyof and typeof are two type-level operators that are often used together:

'keyof T' produces a union of all known public property names (keys) of type T as string literal types.

'typeof value' (in a type context) extracts the TypeScript type of a runtime value. This is different from JavaScript's runtime typeof operator.

Combined pattern: typeof value gives you the type, then keyof typeof value gives you its keys. This is essential for deriving types from runtime objects like config files, constants, and enum-like objects.

codeExamples.title: keyof Operator
codeExamples.explanation: keyof extracts property names as a union of string literals. Combined with generics, it enables type-safe property access where the return type matches the specific key.

codeExamples.title: typeof Operator (Type Context)
codeExamples.explanation: typeof in a type position extracts the compile-time type of a value. It is essential for deriving types from runtime values without manual duplication.

codeExamples.title: Indexed Access Types (Lookup Types)
codeExamples.explanation: Indexed access types let you drill into nested types using bracket notation, just like accessing object properties at runtime. Use [number] to get the element type of an array.

notes: keyof returns string | number | symbol by default, but usually narrows to string literals
typeof in type context is different from JavaScript runtime typeof
The pattern 'typeof obj[keyof typeof obj]' extracts all value types from an object
followUps: What does keyof return for an object with an index signature?
How do indexed access types differ from Pick?
Can you use typeof on a class to get its constructor type?
interviewTips: Show the keyof typeof combo for deriving types from runtime constants
Demonstrate indexed access types for drilling into nested types without redeclaring them
Explain the difference between type-level typeof and runtime typeof

content.question: What are index signatures in TypeScript? How do they compare to the Record utility type?
content.answer:
Index signatures define the type for properties accessed by a dynamic key. They declare that all properties accessed by a certain key type must have a certain value type.

Syntax: { [key: string]: ValueType }

Record<K, V> is a utility type that creates an object type with keys of type K and values of type V. It is implemented using mapped types and is often preferred over index signatures for clarity.

Key differences:
- Index signatures allow any string/number key. Record with a union restricts to specific keys.
- Index signatures mix with explicit properties. Record creates a standalone type.
- Record<string, V> is equivalent to { [key: string]: V }.

codeExamples.title: Index Signatures
codeExamples.explanation: Index signatures handle dynamic keys. When mixing with explicit properties, every explicit property must be assignable to the index signature's value type.

codeExamples.title: Record Utility Type
codeExamples.explanation: Record with union keys creates exhaustive mappings that require all keys. Record with string keys behaves like an index signature but is often more readable.

codeExamples.title: Practical Dictionary Patterns
codeExamples.explanation: Adding '| undefined' to index signatures acknowledges that not every key may be present. This is safer than assuming all lookups succeed.

notes: With noUncheckedIndexedAccess in tsconfig, index signatures return T | undefined by default
Record<string, V> and { [key: string]: V } are equivalent but Record is more concise
Use Record with union keys when you want TypeScript to enforce all keys are present
followUps: What is noUncheckedIndexedAccess and why should you enable it?
How do you type a Map vs a plain object dictionary?
Can index signatures use symbol keys?
interviewTips: Mention noUncheckedIndexedAccess for safer index access - it shows config awareness
Show Record with union keys vs string keys to demonstrate the exhaustiveness difference
Explain the | undefined pattern for honest dictionary typing

content.question: How do classes work with TypeScript's type system? Explain implements, access modifiers, and parameter properties.
content.answer:
TypeScript enhances JavaScript classes with type annotations, access modifiers, and interface implementation.

Key features:
- 'implements' keyword: a class promises to satisfy an interface contract
- Access modifiers: public (default), private, protected, and #private (ES native)
- Parameter properties: shorthand to declare and assign properties in the constructor
- Static members: properties and methods on the class itself, not instances
- Structural typing still applies: a class instance satisfies any interface with a matching shape, even without 'implements'

Classes in TypeScript generate both a runtime value (the constructor function) and a type (the instance shape).

codeExamples.title: Implementing Interfaces
codeExamples.explanation: implements ensures a class fulfills an interface contract at compile time. However, structural typing means any object with the right shape is compatible.

codeExamples.title: Access Modifiers & Parameter Properties
codeExamples.explanation: Parameter properties (public/private/protected/readonly in constructor params) are a shorthand that declares and initializes in one place, reducing boilerplate.

codeExamples.title: Static Members & Class as Type
codeExamples.explanation: A class name used as a type refers to the instance shape. Use 'typeof ClassName' to refer to the constructor. This distinction is important for factory patterns.

notes: TypeScript's private is compile-time only; use #field for true runtime privacy (ES2022)
Parameter properties only work in constructors, not in regular methods
typeof ClassName gives the constructor type; ClassName alone gives the instance type
followUps: What is the difference between TypeScript's private and JavaScript's #private?
How does structural typing affect class compatibility?
When would you use a class vs a plain object with an interface?
interviewTips: Explain that classes generate both a runtime value and a compile-time type
Show parameter properties as a best practice for reducing boilerplate
Mention the structural typing caveat: implements is not required for compatibility

content.question: What are abstract classes in TypeScript? How do they differ from interfaces and when should you use each?
content.answer:
Abstract classes are base classes that cannot be instantiated directly. They can contain both implemented methods (shared logic) and abstract methods (contracts subclasses must fulfill).

Key differences from interfaces:
- Abstract classes can have implementation (method bodies, property initializers)
- Abstract classes can have constructors, access modifiers, and static members
- A class can only extend ONE abstract class but implement MANY interfaces
- Abstract classes produce runtime JavaScript code; interfaces are erased

Use abstract classes when you have shared implementation logic. Use interfaces when you only need a contract with no shared code.

codeExamples.title: Abstract Class Basics
codeExamples.explanation: Abstract classes combine contract enforcement (abstract methods) with code reuse (concrete methods). Subclasses must implement all abstract members.

codeExamples.title: Abstract vs Interface
codeExamples.explanation: The abstract BaseLogger provides error() and warn() implementations. Subclasses only need to implement log(). An interface would force every class to implement all three methods.

codeExamples.title: Template Method Pattern
codeExamples.explanation: The Template Method pattern is a classic use case for abstract classes. The base class defines the algorithm, subclasses provide the steps.

notes: Abstract classes can have constructors; interfaces cannot
A class can extend only one abstract class but implement many interfaces
Abstract classes emit JavaScript code; interfaces are fully erased at compile time
followUps: When would you choose an abstract class over an interface?
Can an abstract class implement an interface?
How does TypeScript handle abstract properties vs abstract methods?
interviewTips: Use the 'shared implementation' argument to justify abstract classes over interfaces
Show the Template Method pattern as a concrete use case
Mention the single-inheritance limitation: only one extends, but multiple implements

content.question: What are declaration files (.d.ts) in TypeScript? How do they enable TypeScript to work with JavaScript libraries?
content.answer:
Declaration files (.d.ts) contain only type information with no runtime code. They describe the shape of JavaScript libraries so TypeScript can type-check code that uses them.

Sources of declarations:
1. Generated by tsc (declaration: true in tsconfig) - published alongside compiled JS
2. DefinitelyTyped (@types/xxx packages) - community-maintained types for JS libraries
3. Hand-written ambient declarations for custom or untyped code

Declaration files use 'declare' keyword for ambient declarations - telling TypeScript that something exists at runtime without providing implementation. They are essential for the TypeScript ecosystem because they allow gradual adoption without rewriting libraries.

codeExamples.title: Writing Declaration Files
codeExamples.explanation: declare module tells TypeScript what types a module exports. This lets you use JavaScript libraries with full type safety and autocompletion.

codeExamples.title: Ambient Declarations & Global Types
codeExamples.explanation: declare global extends existing global types. This is commonly used to type environment variables and window properties injected by build tools.

codeExamples.title: Using @types and DefinitelyTyped
codeExamples.explanation: DefinitelyTyped (@types/*) is the largest collection of community-maintained type declarations. For untyped libraries, write a minimal .d.ts shim and improve it incrementally.

notes: Declaration files contain zero runtime code - they are fully erased during compilation
DefinitelyTyped hosts over 8000 type packages at @types/* on npm
Use 'declare global' to augment Window, process.env, and other global types
followUps: How does TypeScript resolve types for node_modules packages?
What is the difference between typeRoots and types in tsconfig?
How do you publish a library with bundled type declarations?
interviewTips: Explain the DefinitelyTyped ecosystem and @types packages - shows real-world awareness
Show how to type environment variables with declare global - it is a common task
Mention that declaration: true in tsconfig generates .d.ts files from your own code

content.question: What is module augmentation in TypeScript? How do you extend existing library types without modifying their source?
content.answer:
Module augmentation lets you add new declarations to existing modules without touching the original source code. It works because TypeScript supports declaration merging: when two declarations of the same name exist, they are merged.

Common use cases:
1. Adding properties to Express Request/Response
2. Extending third-party library types with plugin methods
3. Adding global utility types or variables
4. Patching incomplete or incorrect library types

Syntax: Use 'declare module "module-name"' to augment an external module. The augmentation must be in a module file (has import/export).

codeExamples.title: Augmenting Express Types
codeExamples.explanation: Augmenting Express is one of the most common real-world uses. By extending the Request interface, your auth middleware and route handlers share type-safe user data.

codeExamples.title: Extending Third-Party Libraries
codeExamples.explanation: Module augmentation and interface merging allow you to add types to libraries without forking them. This is critical for typed middleware, plugins, and theme systems.

codeExamples.title: Namespace & Global Augmentation
codeExamples.explanation: Global augmentation uses 'declare global' inside a module file. It can extend built-in types like Array and add global functions. Use sparingly as it affects the entire project.

notes: Module augmentations must be in a file with imports or exports (a module, not a script)
You can only add new declarations in augmentations, not override existing ones
Interface merging works across files: same-name interfaces are automatically merged
followUps: What is the difference between module augmentation and ambient module declaration?
Can you remove or override a property from a library type via augmentation?
How does declaration merging work for namespaces vs interfaces?
interviewTips: The Express Request augmentation is the most practical example - interviewers expect it
Explain that augmentation only adds, it cannot remove or change existing declarations
Mention that the file must have an import/export to be treated as a module

content.question: What are decorators in TypeScript? Explain the different types and their use cases.
content.answer:
Decorators are special functions that can modify classes, methods, properties, and parameters at declaration time. They use the @expression syntax placed before the target.

TypeScript has two decorator systems:
1. Legacy/Experimental decorators (experimentalDecorators: true) - used by Angular, NestJS, TypeORM
2. TC39 Stage 3 decorators (TypeScript 5.0+) - the standard going forward

Decorator types:
- Class decorators: modify or replace the class constructor
- Method decorators: wrap or modify method behavior
- Property decorators: add metadata to properties
- Parameter decorators: add metadata to method parameters

Decorators enable declarative patterns: logging, validation, dependency injection, ORM mappings, and API route definitions.

codeExamples.title: Class and Method Decorators
codeExamples.explanation: Method decorators receive the target, method name, and property descriptor. They can wrap the original method to add cross-cutting behavior like logging, timing, or caching.

codeExamples.title: Real-World: NestJS-Style Decorators
codeExamples.explanation: Frameworks like NestJS and Angular use decorators heavily for declarative routing, dependency injection, and metadata. The decorator stores metadata that the framework reads at startup.

codeExamples.title: TC39 Stage 3 Decorators (TS 5.0+)
codeExamples.explanation: TC39 Stage 3 decorators are type-safe by design, using context objects instead of reflect-metadata. They are the future standard and work without experimentalDecorators.

notes: Legacy decorators require experimentalDecorators: true in tsconfig
TC39 Stage 3 decorators (TS 5.0+) are the standard going forward
Decorators execute bottom-up for methods and top-down for classes
followUps: What is the difference between legacy and TC39 Stage 3 decorators?
How does the reflect-metadata library work with decorators?
What frameworks heavily rely on decorators?
interviewTips: Mention real frameworks: NestJS, Angular, and TypeORM all rely on decorators
Show both legacy and TC39 decorator syntax to demonstrate awareness of the transition
Explain the metadata pattern: decorators store config, framework reads it at runtime

content.question: How do you use TypeScript effectively with React? Show typing patterns for components, hooks, and event handlers.
content.answer:
TypeScript and React work together to provide type-safe components, hooks, and event handling. Key patterns:

- Props: Use interfaces or type aliases for component props
- useState: TypeScript infers from initial value, or use explicit generic for complex types
- useRef: Use generic for DOM element refs (HTMLDivElement, etc.)
- Events: Use React's typed event types (ChangeEvent, MouseEvent, etc.)
- Children: Use React.ReactNode for the children prop
- Generic components: Components that work with any data type while maintaining type safety

Avoid using React.FC - prefer plain function declarations with typed props for simpler, more predictable types.

codeExamples.title: Component Props & Children
codeExamples.explanation: Type props as interfaces with explicit children: ReactNode. Discriminated unions in props enable conditional rendering with type safety.

codeExamples.title: Hooks Typing
codeExamples.explanation: useState infers from the initial value. Use explicit generics when the initial value is null or does not represent all possible states. useRef<Element>(null) for DOM refs.

codeExamples.title: Event Handlers & Generic Components
codeExamples.explanation: Use React's typed event types for handlers. Generic components accept a type parameter that flows through props, providing type safety without manual annotations.

notes: Prefer plain function declarations over React.FC for simpler types and explicit children
useState<Type | null>(null) is the standard pattern for data loaded asynchronously
Generic components infer T from props, so callers rarely need to specify the type parameter
followUps: What is the difference between React.FC and a plain function component?
How do you type a custom hook that returns multiple values?
How do you type a React context with TypeScript?
interviewTips: Show discriminated union props for conditional rendering - it is a strong pattern
Demonstrate generic components like List<T> to show advanced React + TS integration
Mention avoiding React.FC and explain why (implicit children, no generic support)

content.question: How do you use TypeScript with Node.js and Express? Show patterns for typed middleware, routes, and error handling.
content.answer:
TypeScript with Node.js/Express provides type safety across your entire backend. Key patterns:

- Install @types/node and @types/express for base types
- Type Request with generics: Request<Params, ResBody, ReqBody, Query>
- Augment Express types to add custom properties (user, requestId)
- Create typed middleware with proper next() signatures
- Use generic response wrappers for consistent API responses

The main challenge is typing middleware chains where each middleware adds properties to the request. Module augmentation and generic request types solve this.

codeExamples.title: Typed Express Routes
codeExamples.explanation: Express Request accepts generics for Params, Response Body, Request Body, and Query. This provides autocompletion and type checking for all parts of the request/response cycle.

codeExamples.title: Typed Middleware
codeExamples.explanation: Module augmentation adds custom properties to Request. Middleware functions use the standard (req, res, next) signature. Factory middleware (authorize) returns a closure.

codeExamples.title: Typed Error Handling
codeExamples.explanation: Custom error classes with static factory methods provide clean, typed error creation. The global error handler uses the 4-parameter Express signature for centralized error handling.

notes: Express error handlers must have exactly 4 parameters: (err, req, res, next)
Use module augmentation for adding user, session, or other custom properties to Request
Consider using a wrapper like express-async-errors to avoid try/catch in every route
followUps: How do you type Express middleware that modifies the request object?
What is the difference between @types/express and express's built-in types?
How do you handle async errors in Express with TypeScript?
interviewTips: Show the Request<Params, ResBody, ReqBody, Query> generic pattern
Demonstrate module augmentation for Express Request - it is a must-know for backend TS
Explain the 4-parameter error handler and why the signature matters

content.question: How do you constrain generic types in TypeScript? Explain extends constraints, default types, and when to use them.
content.answer:
Generic constraints restrict what types can be passed as type arguments. Without constraints, a generic T could be anything. With constraints (T extends SomeType), T must be assignable to SomeType.

Key patterns:
- Simple constraint: <T extends object> (T must be an object)
- Property constraint: <T extends { length: number }> (T must have length)
- Key constraint: <K extends keyof T> (K must be a key of T)
- Multiple constraints: <T extends A & B> (T must satisfy both A and B)
- Default types: <T = string> (T defaults to string if not specified)

Constraints make generics more useful by enabling property access and method calls on the constrained type.

codeExamples.title: Basic Constraints with extends
codeExamples.explanation: Constraints tell TypeScript what capabilities T has. Without them, you cannot access any properties. The extends keyword limits T to types that have the required shape.

codeExamples.title: Multiple Constraints & Default Types
codeExamples.explanation: Use intersection (&) to require multiple constraints. Default type parameters provide sensible fallbacks, reducing boilerplate for common use cases.

codeExamples.title: Constrained Generic Patterns
codeExamples.explanation: Constraints ensure generic functions only accept valid types. Recursive constraints model tree structures where nodes reference their own type.

notes: Constraints use extends, not implements, even when constraining to an interface
Default type parameters must come after required type parameters
Recursive constraints (T extends SomeType<T>) are useful for self-referencing structures
followUps: What is the difference between <T extends object> and <T extends {}>?
Can you constrain a generic to be a specific class or its subclasses?
How do constraints interact with conditional types?
interviewTips: Show the keyof constraint pattern for type-safe property access - it is a classic
Demonstrate default type parameters to show you understand API design
Explain that extends in generics means 'is assignable to', not class inheritance

content.question: What are recursive types in TypeScript? Show practical examples of self-referencing type definitions.
content.answer:
Recursive types are types that reference themselves in their definition. They model data structures that are inherently recursive: trees, nested objects, linked lists, and JSON.

TypeScript supports recursion in:
- Type aliases: type Tree<T> = { value: T; children: Tree<T>[] }
- Conditional types: type Deep<T> = T extends object ? { [K in keyof T]: Deep<T[K]> } : T
- Template literal types: type Path = ... (with depth limits)

Recursive conditional types (TS 4.1+) enable deep transformations like DeepPartial, DeepReadonly, and type-safe dot-path access.

codeExamples.title: Recursive Data Structures
codeExamples.explanation: Recursive types naturally model hierarchical data. The key is that the type references itself as a property type, creating potentially infinite nesting.

codeExamples.title: Deep Utility Types
codeExamples.explanation: Deep utility types use recursive conditional types to transform nested structures. DeepPartial is essential for config merging and partial updates of complex objects.

codeExamples.title: Recursive Type-Level Computation
codeExamples.explanation: Recursive types with template literals enable type-safe dot-notation access. The Paths type generates all valid paths, and GetByPath resolves the type at a given path.

notes: TypeScript has a recursion depth limit (typically around 50 levels) for conditional types
Recursive types are lazy: TypeScript only evaluates them as needed during type checking
DeepPartial and DeepReadonly are among the most commonly needed custom utility types
followUps: What is the recursion depth limit for conditional types in TypeScript?
How do you handle circular references in recursive types?
Can you create a type-safe deepGet function using recursive types?
interviewTips: Implement DeepPartial from scratch - it is the most commonly asked recursive type question
Show the JSON type definition as a clean example of self-referencing types
Mention the recursion depth limit to show awareness of practical constraints

content.question: What are the most important tsconfig.json options? Explain strict mode and how compiler settings affect your code.
content.answer:
tsconfig.json configures the TypeScript compiler. The most impactful settings are:

strict: true - enables all strict type checking options:
- strictNullChecks: null/undefined are distinct types, not assignable to other types
- strictFunctionTypes: stricter function parameter checking
- strictPropertyInitialization: class properties must be initialized
- noImplicitAny: error when TypeScript cannot infer a type
- noImplicitThis: error when 'this' has an implicit 'any' type

target: which JS version to compile to (ES5, ES2020, ESNext)
module: module system (CommonJS, ESNext, NodeNext)
moduleResolution: how imports are resolved (node, bundler, nodenext)
paths: alias mappings for imports (@/ to src/)

Always use strict: true for new projects. It catches the most bugs.

codeExamples.title: Essential tsconfig Settings
codeExamples.explanation: This configuration enables maximum type safety with strict: true and noUncheckedIndexedAccess. Path aliases make imports cleaner. The bundler module resolution works with modern build tools.

codeExamples.title: What strict: true Enables
codeExamples.explanation: Each strict flag catches a different category of bugs. strictNullChecks alone prevents the 'billion dollar mistake' of null reference errors.

codeExamples.title: Module Resolution & Target
codeExamples.explanation: target controls JS output version. moduleResolution controls how imports are resolved. Path aliases need matching config in both tsconfig and your build tool.

notes: Always start new projects with strict: true - retroactively enabling it is painful
noUncheckedIndexedAccess is not included in strict but is highly recommended
skipLibCheck: true speeds up compilation by not type-checking node_modules .d.ts files
followUps: What is the difference between moduleResolution 'bundler' and 'nodenext'?
How does the lib option affect available APIs?
What does isolatedModules do and when is it needed?
interviewTips: Know what strict: true includes and be able to name specific flags
Mention noUncheckedIndexedAccess as an extra safety measure beyond strict
Explain the relationship between tsconfig paths and bundler aliases

content.question: How would you migrate a large JavaScript codebase to TypeScript? What is the recommended strategy?
content.answer:
Migrating a large JS codebase to TypeScript should be done incrementally, not all at once. The recommended strategy:

1. Setup phase: Add tsconfig with allowJs: true and checkJs: false. Both .js and .ts files coexist.
2. Infrastructure first: Migrate config files, build scripts, and utilities first.
3. Strict at the boundary: Enable strict for new .ts files, allow loose typing for existing .js files.
4. Bottom-up migration: Start with leaf modules (no dependencies), then work up to modules with more dependencies.
5. Type coverage tracking: Gradually increase coverage, aiming for 100% strict over time.

Key enablers: allowJs, checkJs, JSDoc type annotations, @ts-ignore/@ts-expect-error for temporary suppressions.

codeExamples.title: Migration tsconfig Setup
codeExamples.explanation: Start with allowJs: true and strict: false. This lets .js and .ts files coexist. Gradually enable strict options as more files are converted.

codeExamples.title: JSDoc Types for JS Files
codeExamples.explanation: JSDoc annotations provide type safety in .js files without renaming. With checkJs: true, TypeScript validates JSDoc types. This is perfect for gradual migration.

codeExamples.title: Migration Workflow
codeExamples.explanation: Migrate bottom-up: utilities first, then services, then routes. Use @ts-expect-error (not @ts-ignore) for known issues so TypeScript errors when the underlying issue is fixed.

notes: Use @ts-expect-error over @ts-ignore: it errors when the suppression becomes unnecessary
The type-coverage npm package tracks your migration progress with a percentage score
Renaming .js to .ts is the actual migration step; everything else is preparation
followUps: What is the difference between @ts-ignore and @ts-expect-error?
How do you handle third-party libraries without types during migration?
What tools help track type coverage progress?
interviewTips: Emphasize the gradual approach: big-bang rewrites fail for large codebases
Mention JSDoc as a bridge: it adds type safety without renaming files
Show awareness of tooling: type-coverage for tracking, checkJs for JS validation
